Developer Tools Browser Extensions xRM MDA

Naming Engine: applies naming rules for tables, columns, forms, web resources, and process artifacts. Productivity Tools: provides record actions, test-data helpers, JSON editing, and reusable templates. Documentation Tools: generates form-level documentation, workflow cross references, relationship diagrams, security views, and Word exports.

The header includes a navigation explorer that searches tables from anywhere in the model-driven application. Selecting an item navigates to the selected table so you can move between metadata surfaces without opening the maker portal navigation manually.

The extension exposes metadata tools in two places: List context: opens metadata tools for the entity behind the current view. Shortcut: Alt+M. Record context: opens metadata tools for the entity behind the currently opened record. Shortcut: Alt+M.

See Naming Engine for rule configuration and supported contexts. See Productivity Tools for shortcuts, record actions, and the JSON object manipulator. See Documentation Tools for generated documentation, diagrams, and security views.
Developer Tools Browser Extensions xRM MDA Productivity Tools

Shortcut Action Alt+F Set a fake value on the current record or fill the full form Alt+F+N Set fake values in a new record Alt+G Toggle god mode Alt+N Show or hide logical names on fields Alt+C Clone the current record Alt+I Copy the current record ID Alt+M Open metadata tools for the current entity

The extension keeps helper state available across pages so repeated testing does not require reopening the popup. Templates can store form values for later reuse on test records. God mode can stay enabled across navigation. Logical name overlays can stay enabled without reopening the extension UI.

The JSON object manipulator opens a record editor for inspecting and changing record payloads directly. It is intended for testing, metadata inspection, and template creation. The screen includes: Form operations for opening existing records or creating new ones. API operations for update and create requests. Relationship helpers. Randomization tools for populating fields. Empty-property insertion. Template storage with save and action tabs.

A typical test flow is: Open a record or create a new one. Use the JSON object manipulator or fake-value shortcut to populate fields. Save the result as a template if the same setup is reused. Reapply the template or randomizer on the next test record.
Developer Tools Browser Extensions xRM MDA Documentation Tools

The form documentation view lists fields on the selected form and cross references additional metadata for each field. The form view supports: Form selection. Filtering by field, tab, and section. Visual indicators for required fields, read-only fields, and audit state. Context badges for related triggers, flows, and processes.

The workflow view shows automations related to the current table and can expand each item to list the triggering attributes. The documentation navigation groups related automation surfaces under the same area: Workflows Business Rules BPFs Plugins Power Automate

The relationship view renders selected relationships as a diagram and supports export for reuse outside the extension. Available actions shown in the UI include: Filtering the related entity types used in the diagram. Limiting the number of rendered nodes. Downloading the diagram as SVG. Downloading the diagram as Draw.io.

The security area shows role access for the selected table and lets the result be filtered by user scope and privilege depth. The matrix exposes privilege columns such as create, read, write, delete, append, append-to, assign, and share. The filter bar supports the security levels shown in the UI: User, Business Unit, Deep, and Organization.
The extension can export the current documentation set to a Word document that includes the collected metadata. This is the same type of source document used to derive these pages.
Developer Tools Browser Extensions xRM MDA Privacy Policy — XrmWorldTools MDA

XrmWorldTools MDA is a browser extension that enhances the Power Apps Model-Driven App Maker Portal (make.powerapps.com and related Dynamics 365 domains). This policy explains what data the extension accesses, how it is used, and how it is stored.
XrmWorldTools MDA does not collect, transmit, or share any personal data or telemetry. The extension operates entirely within your browser.

The extension accesses the following information solely to provide its features: Page DOM content on make.powerapps.com, *.dynamics.com, and *.powerplatform.com — used to detect the current editing context (form, field, web resource, etc.) and apply naming conventions, logical name overlays, and god mode. Xrm SDK objects exposed by the Power Apps runtime — used for record actions such as copying record IDs/URLs, cloning records, filling forms with test data, and generating form documentation. Dataverse Web API (/api/data/v9.2/) on the same origin — used to fetch entity metadata, security role information, and lookup records when randomizing form data. All API calls are made directly from your browser to your own Dynamics 365 environment; no data passes through any third-party server.

The extension uses chrome.storage.sync to persist your configuration (naming rules, theme preference, godmode/show-names toggle states, and fake-fill options). This data is synced across your signed-in Chrome browsers via your Google account, as is standard for Chrome sync storage. No data is stored on any external server controlled by the extension author.

Permission Purpose storage Save and sync your extension configuration activeTab Interact with the currently active tab to apply naming rules and record actions tabs Detect tab navigation to re-apply persistent features (godmode, show names) Host permissions (make.powerapps.com, *.dynamics.com, *.powerplatform.com) Inject content scripts on Power Apps and Dynamics 365 pages
The extension does not communicate with any third-party services, analytics platforms, or external servers. All functionality runs locally in your browser against your own Dynamics 365 environment.
If this policy is updated, the changes will be reflected in the extension repository with an updated date.
If you have questions about this privacy policy, please open an issue in the project repository.
Developer Tools Browser Extensions xRM MDA Naming Convention

Global settings define the publisher prefix used across naming rules. The prefix can be referenced from rule templates through ${globalPrefix}. If the maker page indicates a different publisher prefix than the configured global prefix, the extension shows a warning so the component is not created under the wrong publisher.

Context Source Target Table Display Name Schema Name Column Display Name Schema Name Form tab Tab Label Name Form section Section Label Name Web resource Display Name Name Process Display Name Unique Name
Table rules define how a display name becomes a schema name. A rule can be enabled or disabled per context and can apply a convention, a prefix, a suffix, and a maximum length.

Column rules support both fallback values and data-type-specific overrides. When an override matches the displayed column data type label, its prefix or suffix is applied instead of the fallback value.
Data type keyword Typical use lookup Append id or another relationship suffix yes/no Apply boolean prefixes such as is choice Apply publisher or domain prefixes text Apply default text naming rules

Form rules generate names for form tabs and sections. In addition to convention, prefix, suffix, and max length, form rules support templates with token substitution. Supported template tokens shown in the UI include: ${source} ${source2} ${source3} ${globalPrefix} These tokens let a rule compose names from the current label and previously resolved source segments.

Web resource rules generate both path prefixes and file extensions based on file type. File type keyword Prefix Suffix javascript /scripts/ .js css /css/ .css xsl /xsl/ .xsl html /html/ .html xml /xml/ .xml resx /resx/ .resx gif /images/ .gif ico /images/ .ico jpg /images/ .jpg png /images/ .png svg /images/ .svg

Process rules use the same naming model to generate process unique names from the display name. This keeps process artifacts aligned with the same publisher and convention settings used for tables, columns, and forms.
The engine can be toggled from the popup header without changing saved rules. Rule changes are auto-saved. Default values can be restored from the reset action in each settings screen.

A "source" is the entity that caused the trigger. You define the source entity type in both "Template Group" and "Template Trigger". You can use the source property values in writing "Template Item" definition as { "firstname": "{{source.name}}"}
A "target" is the entity being created/updated and defined in the table "Template Item". The "target" is one of the resulted outcome table records and can be defined in "Template Item" table as
The "env" object provides access to environment variables within your template. You can reference environment variables using the following syntax: { "url": "{{env.xrm_EnvironmentVariableName}}"}
Follow these steps to get up and running with the CLI.
Node.js (for installing Docusaurus if hosting docs yourself) .NET 6 SDK A Power Platform environment and appropriate user credentials

Install via NuGet with the following command: dotnet tool install --global XrmDocumentationGeneratorCoreCLI If you already have it installed and want to update: dotnet tool update --global XrmDocumentationGeneratorCoreCLI
Generate your documentation output with: xrm-docgen --url [https://org.crm.dynamics.com](https://org.crm.dynamics.com) --client-id YOUR_APP_ID --client-secret YOUR_SECRET --output docs/

⛔ Below example will fail if used with power automate (not supported directly by power automate) { "regardingobjectid": "{{ entityreference('contact', [{'emailaddress1','john@example.com'},{'lastname','doe'}]) }}"}
documents.s: Use of Entity Reference for Unique Keys Compatability with Power Automate

documents.t: ⛔ Exception will be thrown since the source was not operated yet { "regardingobjectid": "{{ source.contactid }}", "regardingobjectidtype": "contact"} ✅ However updates works fine { "contactid": "{{ source.contactid }}"}
documents.s: Use of Source ID in Pre (Operation or Validation)

documents.t: Review the Introduction to understand the core concepts. See the Terms and Conditions before using the Template Engine.

Creating a Template Group: Define a group of template items to automate actions on Dataverse records. Use filtering expressions to control when groups run. Defining Template Items: Use the Monaco Editor for intelligent suggestions. Reference source, env, and faker in your expressions. Setting Up Triggers: Configure triggers to run templates on specific events (e.g., record updates). Monitoring Jobs: Check the Template Jobs log for history and troubleshooting.

Templates not running? Ensure triggers are correctly configured and enabled. Check filtering expressions for syntax errors. Unexpected data changes? Test templates in a non-production environment first. Review template logic and conditions. Editor issues? Refresh the page if Monaco Editor suggestions do not appear. Ensure your browser supports modern JavaScript features.

Q: How do I reference fields from the source record? A: Use source.fieldname in your template expressions. Q: Can I use environment variables? A: Yes, use env.variablename in your templates. Q: How do I roll back changes? A: Use the Roll Back feature in the Template Jobs log to delete records created by a job.
Group of template items (set of actions).Defines the scope of actions and the source type. A group can be filtered by a filtering expression.
Runs only if the source state is Active statecode != 1

A table "target" object definition in JSON. { "leadid": "{{ source.leadid }}", "new_expirydate": "{{ !source.address1_line1? (source.new_expirydate ?? date.now | date.add_years 2) : (source.new_expirydate ?? date.now | date.add_years 1) }}", "leadqualitycode": "{{ !source.address1_line1? 1 : 3}}"}

Using the TemplateEngine.Condition property. Runs only if the source owning business unit has country code and the source has an auto number. "TemplateEngine": { "Condition": "{{ source.owningbusinessunit.xrm_countrycode && source.xrm_autonumber }}"}

Table represents plugin processing steps. Each record represents a plugin step. On save, a relationship is created for the polymorphic lookup on the json, if it does not exist, 1:N target record type specified in the trigger record.

Log of triggered groups. Every time an event is triggered by the template trigger definitions. A job is created for history. A job will run a group of items using a plugin step defined in the trigger table or will be executed by a Power Automate flow " [TemplateEngine] Apply Template Job"
Template job is automatically related to the target record initated the job. Therefore you can find all related job runs to a specific record.

Relationships: Group → Items: One-to-Many (1:N) Group → Triggers: Many-to-Many (N:N) Group → Jobs: Many-to-Many (N:N) Triggers → Jobs: Many-to-Many (N:N) Group Structure: Items: One group has many items (1:N) Triggers: Many-to-many relationship with groups (N:N) Jobs: Many-to-many relationship with both groups (N:N) and triggers (N:N)

The editor uses the Monaco Editor, providing intelligent suggestions and auto-completions for template expressions. As you type, the editor offers context-aware completions for properties like source, env, and faker, making it easy to construct dynamic expressions. For example, typing source. will suggest available fields from the source record, while env. provides environment variables, and faker. exposes bogus fake data generation for testing. The schema functionality powers these suggestions, ensuring that only valid properties in all entities metadata and methods are shown based on your template item entity context.
Depending on the {{attribute field}}type field the object autocomplete switches
Mock fake records to automate test scenarios with fake information { "emailaddress1": "{{faker.internet.email()}}"}

On a job. Click "Roll Back" to delete records created by the job. That is useful for testing.

The Template Engine is provided for automating record operations within Microsoft Power Platform environments. You are responsible for ensuring that all templates, triggers, and actions comply with your organization’s data policies and security requirements.

The Template Engine processes and modifies data in Dataverse tables as defined by your templates and triggers. It is your responsibility to validate templates before deployment to avoid unintended data changes or loss. Always test templates in a non-production environment before applying them to live data.

Do not include sensitive or confidential information in templates, triggers, or environment variables unless proper security measures are in place. Access to the Template Engine and its configuration should be restricted to authorized users only.
The authors and contributors of the Template Engine are not liable for any data loss, corruption, or security breaches resulting from its use. Use of the Template Engine is at your own risk.
The Template Engine is provided as-is, without any guarantee of support or future updates. Contributions and suggestions are welcome via the project’s repository.

These terms and conditions may be updated at any time. Continued use of the Template Engine constitutes acceptance of the latest terms. If you do not agree to these terms, do not use the Template Engine.

You can use the entityreference function to look up and reference records by IDs or key field values: Lookup by ID: { "contactid": "{{ entityreference('contact','da509586-a5d2-f011-8543-000d3adcf1d4') }}"} Lookup by 1 unique key: { "contactid": "{{ entityreference('contact','emailaddress1','john@gmail.com') }}"} Lookup by multiple unique keys: { "contactid": "{{ entityreference('contact', [{'emailaddress1': 'john@example.com'}, {'lastname': 'doe'}]) }}"}
One-to-many operations allow you to aggregate or summarize data from related records. This is useful for rollups or creating summaries.

Calculate the total duration of all phone calls related to an opportunity and update a custom field on the opportunity record. { "xrm_totalcallminutes": "{{sum = 0; for item in source.Oppportunity_PhoneCalls; if item.actualdurationminutes; sum = sum + item.actualdurationminutes; end; end; sum}}"}
Create a comma-separated list of subjects for all tasks related to an opportunity. { "xrm_tasksummary": "{{source.Opportunity_Tasks | array.map 'subject' | array.join ', '}}"}

Calculate the total time spent on all cases related to an account and update a custom field on the account record. { "xrm_totalcaseminutes": "{{sum = 0; for item in source.Account_Cases; if item.timespent; sum = sum + item.timespent; end; end; sum}}"}
Create a comma-separated list of case titles for all cases related to an account. { "xrm_casesummary": "{{source.Account_Cases | array.map 'title' | array.join ', '}}"}

{ "new_expirydate": "{{ !source.address1_line1 ? date.now | date.add_years 2 : date.now | date.add_years 1 }}", "new_reminderdate": "{{ date.now | date.add_days 30 }}", "new_followupon": "{{ date.now | date.add_days (6 - date.now.day_of_week) }}"}

{ "new_leadnumber": "{{ 'LEA-' | append source.leadid }}", "telephone1_clean": "{{ source.telephone1 | string.remove ' ' | string.remove '-' }}", "emailaddress1": "{{ source.emailaddress1 | string.default 'no-email@contoso.com' }}"}
You can use the faker function to generate random test data, such as picking a random value from a list: { "countrycode": "{{ faker.pick_random(['US','DK','NL','DE','FR']) }}"}

The Template Engine does not collect or transmit any personal data outside your organization’s environment. All data processed by the Template Engine remains within your Dataverse and Power Platform infrastructure.
The Template Engine processes data as defined by your templates, triggers, and user actions. No data is shared with third parties by the Template Engine itself.
Data security is managed by your organization’s Power Platform and Dataverse security policies. Access to the Template Engine and its configuration should be restricted to authorized users.

Users are responsible for ensuring that templates and triggers do not expose or misuse sensitive information. Always follow your organization’s data protection and privacy guidelines when configuring and using the Template Engine.

This Privacy Policy may be updated at any time. Continued use of the Template Engine constitutes acceptance of the latest policy. If you have questions or concerns about privacy, please contact your system administrator or data protection officer.
Automatically create phone call activities and related records when a booking is confirmed in Field Service scenarios.
When a booking is created or confirmed: Create a confirmation phone call activity Update booking with calculated fields Create a pre-appointment reminder
Template Engine solution installed Bookable Resource Booking entity Phone Call and Task entities

Field Value Name Booking Confirmation Workflow Source Entity bookableresourcebooking Filter Expression statecode == 0 && bookingstatus == 690970001 Filter ensures only confirmed bookings (status 690970001) trigger the workflow

documents.s: Template Item 1: Create Confirmation Phone Call

documents.t: Target Entity: bookableresourcebooking { "bookableresourcebookingid": "{{ source.bookableresourcebookingid }}", "new_confirmationcallscheduled": true, "new_confirmationdate": "{{ date.now }}", "new_estimatedarrival": "{{ source.starttime | date.add_minutes -15 }}"}
documents.s: Template Item 2: Update Booking with Confirmation Details

documents.t: Field Value Name On Booking Confirmed Entity bookableresourcebooking Message Create / Update Filtering Attributes bookingstatus

When a booking is confirmed: Confirmation phone call is created immediately Booking record is updated with confirmation details Pre-appointment reminder task is created (if booking > 1 day away) Post-service follow-up call is scheduled All activities are linked to the booking record
Use Roll Back on the Template Job to remove all created activities during testing.
Model-driven Apps Template Engine Use Cases API Trigger
documents.s: Use Case: API Trigger Job with Resolved JSON Body

documents.t: Generate a resolved JSON payload as a completed Template Job without targeting any specific Dataverse entity. This allows external systems (e.g., Power Automate, Logic Apps, Azure Functions) to trigger the template, receive the fully rendered JSON string, and use it as a request body for API calls or integration flows.

Custom integration on order is assigned a customer, send to ERP. Instead of composing the body and the trigger conditions in Power Automate flow, a template job will execute, resolve the JSON with source (order) data and system functions, and complete—making the output accessible for further processing.
Source entity order No target entity specified (empty entity)
External integration (Power Automate, Logic App, custom middleware) to consume the resolved JSON
Field Value Name API Trigger: Purchase Order JSON Body Source Entity Order table Filter Expression (None)

Target Entity: (Empty — generates resolved JSON only) { "PurchaseOrder": { "OrderNumber": "ordernumber", "CustomerID": "{{source.customerid.new_cnumber}}", "OrderDate": "{{ date.now | date.to_string '%Y-%m-%d' }}", "TotalAmount": 249.48, "Description": "Purchase order for customer {{source.customerid.new_cnumber}}" }} Explanation: A job will be created with the group unique number (a flow can filter runs based on that unique number) No entity type specified → job will complete without any operations executed Uses source.customerid.new_cnumber to inject dynamic customer ID OrderDate is set to today’s date Resolved JSON can be accessed in the completed job output
Enables reusable API payload generation Decouples JSON generation from record creation Simplifies integration with external systems Flexible and extensible for additional JSON structures
Model-driven Apps Template Engine Use Cases Lead Retention Expiry
documents.s: Use Case: Automated Retention Expiry Logic for Leads

documents.t: Automatically update the Retention Expiry Date (new_expirydate) on Lead records based on GDPR policy requirements. This use case is part of an investigation-only initiative to explore how retention expiry could be managed programmatically in Dynamics 365 CE.

To support GDPR compliance and internal retention policy rules, Leads must have a Retention Expiry Date that updates based on: Lead creation date Lead category and applicable retention period Positive actions extending retention Business-unit–specific rules (future investigation) The logic below demonstrates how an automated update could work when a Lead is modified. Since the source record is the target Lead itself, updates are applied directly using source.leadid.

Template Engine solution available Lead entity (lead) Field new_expirydate added (date only) Business logic conditions for determining 1-year vs. 2-year retention Audit trail enabled in Dataverse to track changes to retention fields No production implementation yet — this is an investigation outcome only
Field Value Name Lead Retention Expiry Automation Source Entity lead Filter Expression leadid != null

Target Entity: lead (source is updated record) { "leadid": "{{ source.leadid }}", "new_expirydate": "{{ !source.address1_line1? (source.new_expirydate ?? date.now | date.add_years 2) : (source.new_expirydate ?? date.now | date.add_years 1) }}", "leadqualitycode": "{{ !source.address1_line1? 1 : 3 }}"} Explanation: The record being updated is the same as the source → use leadid = source.leadid If address1_line1 is empty, apply 2-year retention If address1_line1 is present, apply 1-year retention Existing expiry date is kept unless missing Updates leadqualitycode to reflect lead categorization (hot if address is filled in by the lead. Otherwise categorized as cold) This is just a simple example for 1 criteria
documents.s: Template Item: Update Lead Retention Expiry

documents.t: Model-driven Apps Template Engine Use Cases Resource Assignment Call
documents.s: Use Case: Follow-Up Call After Resource Assignment

documents.t: Automatically create a follow-up phone call activity when a booking receives a resource assignment, ensuring timely coordination between the planner and the assigned resource.

When a booking is created with an existing resource: Generate a follow-up call scheduled 2 weeks later Link the phone call to the booking Ensure a reminder exists to verify booking readiness and availability
Group source entity: bookableresourcebooking Item entity: phonecall
Resource entity (resource) must be related to the booking

Target Entity: phonecall { "TemplateEngine": { "Condition": "{{ source.resource }}" }, "description": "Call {{ source.resource.name }}", "regardingobjectid": "{{ entityreference('bookableresourcebooking', source.bookableresourcebookingid) }}", "scheduledend": "{{ date.now | date.add_days 14 | date.to_string '%Y-%m-%d 15:00:00' }}", "subject": "Call {{ source.resource.name }} and book appointment (2 weeks)"} Explanation: Only runs if source.resource contains data via TemplateEngine.Condition Schedules the call exactly 14 days from today at 15:00 Links call to the booking via regardingobjectid Call subject & body dynamically include the assigned resource’s name tip Trigger must be post operation to utilize the source entity ID
documents.s: Template Item: Follow-Up Call After Assignment

documents.t: Field Value Name ⚡On Postoperation of Created Bookable Resource Booking [SYNC] Entity bookableresourcebooking Message Create Stage Post-operation Filtering Attributes resource

When a resource is assigned to a booking: A follow-up call is automatically created The call is linked to the booking The planner is reminded to coordinate with the assigned resource The call appears in the booking’s activity timeline
